title: The July Renewal Pile-Up: The Cash Flow Squeeze Most Australian SMEs Don't See Coming
linkedin:
Your FY26 numbers are filed. Your FY27 budget is set. So why does the bank account tighten again in the third week of July?

It's rarely bad trading. It's a cluster of annual costs that all happen to renew on the same date: 1 July.

Public liability and professional indemnity policies. Workers compensation. Professional memberships and licensing fees. A good share of annual software subscriptions. All anchored to the financial year boundary, all landing inside the same three to four week window.

Then the June quarter BAS payment lands on top, due 28 July. Trade well through April, May and June and the GST liability inside that statement is bigger too, right when the renewal cluster is heaviest.

A business paying fifteen thousand dollars in insurance renewals, eight thousand in subscriptions and a thirty thousand dollar BAS bill inside the same three weeks has committed over fifty thousand dollars in cash before a single invoice for that period has been collected. None of it looks alarming alone. Stacked together, it changes what the business can safely do with its cash.

Here's the part that catches people out. The profit and loss statement never warns you. Accrual accounting spreads these costs evenly across twelve months, correct for measuring profit, but the P&L shows a flat line every month while the cash leaves in one lump sum, on one date.

The fix is simple. Map every known-date cash event, renewals, BAS dates, super payments, onto one forward calendar the moment the new year opens. The cluster stops being a surprise and becomes a known event weeks out.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
Filed your EOFY numbers and finally exhaled? Keep an eye on the third week of July.

Insurance renewals, software subscriptions, professional memberships, they all seem to reset around 1 July. Add the June quarter BAS payment, due the 28th, and suddenly a handful of completely expected bills land in the same fortnight.

None of it shows up as a warning on your profit and loss statement either, since accounting spreads these costs evenly across the year while the cash leaves in one hit.

Has your business felt this squeeze in past Julys? We'd genuinely like to know how you planned around it.

#CashFlow #SMEFinance
google:
A Note for East Coast Business Owners: Watch the Back Half of July

If your EOFY reporting is filed and things feel settled, it's worth checking one date on the calendar before you relax completely: late July.

A lot of annual business costs reset around 1 July. Insurance renewals, professional memberships, licensing fees, and a good share of annual software subscriptions all tend to anchor to the financial year boundary. On their own, each one is entirely expected. Landing in the same three to four week window, together with the June quarter BAS payment due on the 28th, they can create a cash squeeze that catches even well-run businesses by surprise.

The tricky part is that your profit and loss statement won't show this coming. Accrual accounting spreads these costs evenly across the year, which is correct for measuring profit, but it means the cash impact lands as one lump sum rather than twelve even instalments.

The fix is straightforward: map every renewal date, BAS due date and superannuation payment onto a single forward calendar as soon as the new financial year opens. Once it's mapped, the cluster becomes a known event weeks in advance rather than a surprise.

We work with owner-led businesses across Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria on exactly this kind of forward cash planning. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
